frame_label: Women's Safety as Her Own Responsibility
generated_comment:
A: Arre, I told Riya also, office se itna late nikle toh cab details share karke niklo and avoid these parties yaar. Mumbai is better than other cities, but zamaana theek nahi hai.
B: Haan, boys ko toh samjhana chahiye, but practically ladkiyon ko hi extra careful rehna padta hai. Why invite trouble when you can be sensible?
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This is misogynistic because it frames women’s safety as primarily their own responsibility and normalizes male violence as something women must manage. It uses protective, practical advice ('extra careful', 'avoid parties', 'zamaana theek nahi hai') instead of centering accountability for men.
original_frame: Daughter as Financial Burden: Dowry, Marriage Cost, and the Economics of Female Birth
persona_text: You are Vikram. Age 38. Married. A man in Mumbai. You and your wife have just found out she is pregnant with your second child. Your first child is a daughter. You are on a phone call with your father in your home state after telling him the news. Your father is happy about the pregnancy. He asks if you are hoping for a boy this time. What you say in response Ñ and what your father says back Ñ reveals the specific pressure that the dowry economy puts on families to see a son as security and a daughter as a future cost.
